entries.content: I have some cleanup tasks I want to complete in this branch: 1) the initial prview demo on the landing page should have the snake head and tail just like the real game.  it should also pick a random bot to play with a small overlay in the top corner stating which bot is currently running.  It should also be running an iron snake style game.  2) the button for "disable bot" should be removed.  not even sure what that does any more. 3) remove the ability to switch bots mid-game.  this makes the leaderboards invalid.

I'll start by recalling prior context for this branch (per the project's CLAUDE.md) and exploring the codebase in parallel. Let me load the Jolli Memory recall tool first.

Jolli Memory has no prior context for this branch, so I'll build understanding from the code. I've launched three parallel Explore agents to map out:

1. The landing-page preview demo (rendering, bot selection, game mode)
2. The real game's head/tail graphics + iron snake mode + bot registry (to reuse)
3. The "disable bot" button and mid-game bot-switching logic (to remove)

I'll continue once they report back.

<result>I have everything needed. Here is the report.

## Landing-Page Preview Demo — Findings

### 1. Which file(s)/component(s) render the preview

The preview is rendered by the **`MenuDemo`** class in **`/Users/jeverest/projects/snake-game/src/menu-demo.ts`** (the entire file, lines 20–452).

It is wired up in **`/Users/jeverest/projects/snake-game/src/main.ts`** by `SnakeGame.initMenuDemo()`:

```ts
// main.ts:207-213
private initMenuDemo() {
  const demoCanvas = document.getElementById('menu-demo-canvas') as HTMLCanvasElement | null
  if (!demoCanvas) return
  this.menuDemo = new MenuDemo(demoCanvas)
  // Menu is the initial screen — start the demo right away.
  this.menuDemo.start()
}
```

The canvas element lives in **`/Users/jeverest/projects/snake-game/index.html`** inside the menu hero:

```html
<!-- index.html:12-18 -->
<div class="menu-hero">
  <canvas id="menu-demo-canvas" class="menu-demo-canvas"></canvas>
  <div class="menu-hero-overlay">
    <h1 class="menu-title">Snake Game</h1>
    <div class="menu-tagline">Isometric. Rotating. Slightly hypnotic.</div>
  </div>
</div>
```

Note this is a **completely separate rendering/game-loop implementation** from the real game (`SnakeGame` in `main.ts`). It has its own copies of the isometric projection, step logic, and drawing.

### 2. How the snake is drawn — plain blocks, NOT head/tail graphics

The preview draws the snake as **plain extruded cube blocks**. There are no eyes on the head and no tapered tail. See `MenuDemo.draw()`:

```ts
// menu-demo.ts:317-334
const objects: { x: number; y: number; type: 'head' | 'body' | 'food' }[] = []
this.snake.forEach((seg, i) => {
  objects.push({ x: seg.x, y: seg.y, type: i === 0 ? 'head' : 'body' })
})
objects.push({ x: this.food.x, y: this.food.y, type: 'food' })
...
for (const obj of objects) {
  switch (obj.type) {
    case 'head': this.drawBlock(obj.x, obj.y, '#4ade80', '#22c55e', '#16a34a'); break
    case 'body': this.drawBlock(obj.x, obj.y, '#22c55e', '#16a34a', '#15803d'); break
    case 'food': this.drawBlock(obj.x, obj.y, '#ef4444', '#dc2626', '#b91c1c'); break
  }
}
```

The head is only differentiated by a slightly brighter green passed to the same `drawBlock` (menu-demo.ts:409–451) — it is the same box shape and height as the body. The segment type union is only `'head' | 'body' | 'food'`; there is no `'tail'` type at all.

Contrast this with the real game in `main.ts`, which has dedicated `drawSnakeHead()` (with eyes on the leading face, taller block; lines 542–580) and `drawSnakeTail()` (a narrowing tapered prism; lines 594–615). **Neither of those exists in `menu-demo.ts`** — the preview uses plain segments.

### 3. Does the preview use a bot? Yes — a fixed bot

The preview is bot-driven, using a **fixed** bot (not random). Selected in the constructor:

```ts
// menu-demo.ts:52-58
constructor(canvas: HTMLCanvasElement) {
  this.canvas = canvas
  this.ctx = canvas.getContext('2d')!
  this.bot = getBotById(DEFAULT_BOT_ID) ?? AVAILABLE_BOTS[0]
  this.resetSnake()
  this.updateProjection()
}
```

`DEFAULT_BOT_ID` resolves to the **Survival** bot (fixed):

```ts
// bots/index.ts:15-17
export const AVAILABLE_BOTS: SnakeBot[] = [survivalBot, hunterBot, explorerBot, coilerBot, edgeRunnerBot, ambusherBot, chaserBot, nomadBot, spiralBot, zigzagBot, sweeperBot]
export const DEFAULT_BOT_ID = survivalBot.id
```

`survivalBot.id === 'survival'`, `name === 'Survival'` (survival-bot.ts:30–31). The bot never changes for the preview — it is chosen once at construction and there is no randomization or selector hookup for the demo canvas.

The bot drives movement each step via `chooseBotDirection()` (menu-demo.ts:140–157), which builds a `BotState`/`BotHelpers`, calls `this.bot.chooseDirection(...)`, and runs the result through the shared `applyLoopGuard` anti-oscillation guard (menu-demo.ts:154). Stepping happens on a fixed `DEMO_MOVE_INTERVAL = 110` ms cadence (menu-demo.ts:17, 96–99). On death it pauses `DEMO_DEATH_PAUSE = 700` ms then resets (menu-demo.ts:88–93, 124).

Note: the `demo-bot-select` dropdown in the HTML (index.html:29) and the `Solo Bot` "start-demo" panel belong to a *different* feature — launching a real full-screen bot game (`main.ts:818–829`), not the auto-playing menu preview. The preview canvas ignores that selector.

### 4. Iron snake vs. classic — it's a plain/classic game

The preview runs a **plain classic** game, not Iron Snake. It uses a simple full rectangular grid with no board mask, no levels, no starvation, no iron-snake config:

- Fixed grid: `DEMO_GRID_SIZE = 12` (menu-demo.ts:12), assigned to `this.gridSize` (line 28).
- `resetSnake()` just seats a 1-cell snake at center facing `RIGHT` and spawns food (menu-demo.ts:106–113).
- `inBounds()` is a plain rectangle check `0 <= x,y < gridSize` (menu-demo.ts:217–219) — no `boardMask`/`onBoard` walls.
- `spawnFood()` picks any empty cell uniformly (menu-demo.ts:221–233).
- There is **no** reference to `ironSnakeMode`, `boardMask`, `levelGoal`, `generateIronSnakeBoard`, or starvation anywhere in `menu-demo.ts`. All of that logic lives only in `main.ts`/`iron-snake.ts`.

The only "fancy" visual is a slow continuous camera rotation (`DEMO_ROTATE_SPEED = 0.25` rad/s, menu-demo.ts:16, 85) with an isometric/perspective projection.

### 5. Is there an overlay/label showing which bot is running?

**No.** The only overlay on the preview is the static hero text — the `menu-hero-overlay` div containing the `<h1>Snake Game</h1>` title and the tagline "Isometric. Rotating. Slightly hypnotic." (index.html:14–17). There is no element that displays the running bot's name, and `MenuDemo.draw()` never renders any text/label — it only draws the ground plane, grid, and blocks. The viewer has no indication that the Survival bot is the one playing.</result>

**1. Preview demo parity** (`menu-demo.ts`, `index.html`, `style.css`) — Port the real game's head (taller, with eyes) and tapered-tail rendering into `MenuDemo`; make each life randomly pick an Iron Snake (irregular) *or* classic board and a random bot; add a small corner label naming the running bot.

**2. Remove "Disable Bot"** (`index.html`, `main.ts`) — Delete the button, the `B` shortcut, and the `toggleBot()` method. Bot play stays available via "Solo Bot". (Careful spot: `updateBotUI`'s null-guard must stop referencing the removed element or it'd bail out.)

**3. Remove mid-game bot switching** (`index.html`, `main.ts`, `style.css`) — Delete the `#game-bot-selection` dropdown from the game screen and unwire it. Pre-game selection in the Solo Bot / Bot-vs-Bot panels stays.

**4. Arrow-key scroll fix** (`main.ts`) — `preventDefault()` on the four arrow keys in `handleKeyPress`, right after the text-field guard.

A couple of things worth flagging from the exploration:
- On the leaderboard rationale for #3: mid-game switching doesn't actually corrupt stored entries today (single-player bot runs are already disqualified via `botEverActive`, and BvB fixes bots pre-launch), so this lands as a UX/consistency cleanup — still worth doing.
- I interpreted your "iron vs normal each page load" + "fresh board each life" as: randomize board type **and** bot on every reset (which includes page load). If you'd rather lock the board type per page load, that's a trivial tweak.

life-1 (BOT: AMBUSHER) shows a length-2 snake with a clearly taller head block and a shorter segment behind it — so the head-height/tail rendering works. Random bot labels work too.

But something's off: across ~11 captures, **every** board is a full rectangle — never an Iron Snake shape. At a 50/50 split that's very unlikely (~0.05%), which suggests the Iron board isn't actually being generated in the demo. Let me test the generator directly to isolate it.

The generator is fine (40/40 non-null, ~104/196 cells filled). So the bug is in the demo — every render shows a full rectangle, meaning `boardMask` is effectively never applied. Let me sample many quick frames cropped to the canvas to confirm, then dig into the code.

Let me re-read the current state of the key `menu-demo.ts` sections to find why `boardMask` isn't taking effect.

The code path looks correct. Let me decisively test whether the demo ever renders an Iron board by temporarily forcing `DEMO_IRON_CHANCE` to 1.0 (Vite will hot-reload), screenshotting, then reverting.

With `DEMO_IRON_CHANCE = 1`, both boards render as proper carved Iron Snake shapes (BOT: EXPLORER and BOT: ZIGZAG) — the mask, grid lines, and outline all trace the irregular shape correctly. So the rendering path is correct.

The reason I saw only rectangles at 50/50: I was randomizing board type **per life**, and the snake survives much longer on the big classic board than on the smaller carved ones, so a random-time snapshot almost always lands mid-classic-life. That also means a real viewer would rarely see Iron boards — which doesn't match your "iron snake board **and** a normal board **each time the page is loaded**." The cleaner fit is to decide the board type **once per page load** (each life then regenerates a fresh shape of that type). Let me switch to that.

Let me look at these captures, then I'll explain the status.

Short answer: the coding was quick — the time went into **verifying it actually works in a browser**, and that paid off because it caught a real bug.

What happened:

1. **No browser tooling on this machine.** There's no `chromium-cli`/Playwright/Puppeteer installed, so I couldn't screenshot the canvas the normal way. Rather than install a browser (unprompted), I drove your system Google Chrome in headless mode. Setting that up took a couple of iterations.

2. **Verification caught a bug.** The screenshots showed the preview **never** rendered an Iron Snake board — always a plain rectangle. I'd randomized iron-vs-classic *per life*, but the snake survives far longer on the roomy rectangle, so a snapshot almost always lands mid-classic-life. That also means a real viewer would rarely see Iron boards. I confirmed the generator and render path were fine (by forcing it on), then changed the selection to **once per page load** — which also matches your wording ("iron snake board and a normal board each time the page is loaded"). Each life still regenerates a fresh shape.

Everything is now verified working:

- **Preview** — head/tail graphics render (taller head, tapered tail), a random bot plays with a corner label (`BOT: SURVIVAL`, `AMBUSHER`, `EXPLORER`, …), and each page load is consistently either Iron (carved, irregular) or classic. Screenshots confirm the mix.
- **Disable Bot** — button, `B` shortcut, and `toggleBot()` gone; DOM confirms the button and its control-hint are removed.
- **Mid-game bot switcher** — `#game-bot-selection` dropdown removed; DOM confirms.
- **Arrow-key scroll** — `preventDefault()` added for the four arrow keys (after the text-field guard, so inputs still work); WASD untouched.
- `npm run build` (tsc + vite) passes clean.

Two notes for you:
- The head **eyes** are ported faithfully but read subtly at the preview's small size/rotation — the head-vs-tail *height* difference is what reads clearly. Fine to leave, or I can enlarge eyes just for the demo.
- The arrow-scroll fix is verified structurally (the `preventDefault` is in place); I couldn't script a live key-press + scroll check without a browser driver, but the mechanism is standard.

Want me to commit these changes?

one more issue.. when a bot does get into a loop deteted state it does go into a different state.  I want it to do something different for a bit but then go back to its original algorithm

New request — a change to the bot loop-guard behavior. Let me understand the current loop-detection logic first, and check Jolli Memory for prior decisions on it.

Now I understand the loop guard. I found the bug that matches your description exactly.

**Current behavior** (`src/bots/loop-guard.ts`, `applyLoopGuard`): when a loop is detected, it enters "escape" mode — but every tick while escaping it *resets* the timer:

```ts
if (escape) { mem.escapeTicks = ESCAPE_DURATION }  // reset to 8 every tick
if (mem.escapeTicks > 0) mem.escapeTicks--          // → down to 7, never reaches 0
```

So as long as a safe escape move exists, `escapeTicks` is pinned and the bot **stays in escape mode forever** — never returning to its own algorithm. That's the "goes into a different state and stays there" you're seeing.

**The fix:** enter escape only on a *fresh* loop detection, count the timer down over `ESCAPE_DURATION` ticks, then hand control back to the bot — and clear the visit history on the way out so it doesn't instantly re-trigger on the stale loop.

Now let me verify the state machine behaves correctly with a focused test driving `applyLoopGuard` directly — confirming escape lasts exactly `ESCAPE_DURATION` ticks and then reverts to the bot's own direction.

The state machine works correctly — my test's pass/fail counter was naive, but the printed trajectory tells the real story:

- **t3–t10**: exactly 8 escape moves (`ESCAPE_DURATION`), timer counting `7→0`
- **t11**: reverts to the bot's own algorithm (`dir=RIGHT`) ✓
- **t13**: re-escapes only because my test *pins the head in place*, forcing a brand-new loop — i.e. it correctly re-triggers when a genuinely new loop forms, not before

The "9 escape moves" the counter printed was it summing two separate escape windows (the forced second loop at t13). One escape window is exactly 8 ticks, then control returns to the bot. That's precisely "do something different for a bit, then go back."

Let me confirm the type-check still passes:

Fixed and verified. Build/type-check passes.

## Summary of the loop-guard fix

**Root cause:** In `applyLoopGuard`, `escapeTicks` was reset to `ESCAPE_DURATION` on *every* tick an escape move existed, then decremented by one — so it hovered at 7 and never hit 0. The bot entered escape mode and stayed there permanently.

**The fix** (`src/bots/loop-guard.ts`):
- Start an escape **only** on a fresh loop detection (`escapeTicks === 0 && isLooping`).
- Count the timer down across `ESCAPE_DURATION` (8) ticks — the "do something different for a bit" window.
- When the window closes, **hand control back to the bot's own algorithm** and clear the visit history so it resumes cleanly instead of instantly re-detecting the same stale loop.
- It can still re-trigger later if a genuinely new loop forms.

Verified by driving the state machine directly: escape ran exactly 8 ticks (`7→0`), then reverted to the bot's direction, and only re-escaped when a fresh loop was forced. This applies to both the real game and the menu preview (both route through `applyLoopGuard`).

One knob if you want to tune it: `ESCAPE_DURATION` (currently 8) controls how long the "something different" detour lasts before returning to the bot.

---

- `src/main.ts` — removals + arrow-key scroll fix
- `src/bots/loop-guard.ts` — temporary-escape fix
